coredata.dc:title: A dual-mode ratiometric fluorescence and smartphone-assisted colorimetric sensing platform based on bifunctional Fe,Co-CQD for glucose analysis at physiological pH
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Linjie
Zheng, Shujun
Lu, Ling
Li, Caolong
Wang, Fei
coredata.dc:description: Diabetes is a common and fatal chronic metabolic disease characterized by hyperglycemia, and thus monitoring blood glucose level is essential for early screening and timely control of disease. Herein, we have prepared the bifunctional iron and cobalt co-doped carbon quantum dot (Fe,Co-CQD) with good optical properties and peroxidase-mimetic catalytic activity toward specific substrate o-phenylenediamine (OPD) under alkaline condition. Glucose oxidase (GOx) specifically catalyzes the oxidation of glucose into H2O2, and Fe,Co-CQD subsequently triggers a reaction between H2O2 and OPD to form yellow product DAP with a distinct UV absorption peak at 420 nm. Simultaneously, the generated DAP also appears a well-defined fluorescence signal at 555 nm, which can suppress the intrinsic fluorescence peak of Fe,Co-CQD (439 nm) owing to the inner filter effect (IFE). Based on this principle, a dual-mode ratiometric fluorescence and colorimetric sensing platform has been constructed for glucose analysis at physiological pH, which reveals the advantages of excellent accuracy, high throughput, simple operation, and low cost. More importantly, a smartphone-assisted colorimetric sensing system based on a portable visual detection kit and a 3D printing smartphone-based device has been constructed, which enables on-site detection of glucose in complex serum samples without laboratory instruments, indicating its potential practical application prospect.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Blood glucose
Fe,Co-CQD
Peroxidase-mimetic catalytic activity
Dual-mode
Smartphone-assisted colorimetric sensing system

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of iron and cobalt co-doped carbon quantum dot (Fe,Co-CQD)
Investigation on the peroxidase-like catalytic performance of Fe,Co-CQD
Colorimetric and fluorescence detection of H2O2 and glucose
Fabrication of portable detection kits and smartphone-assisted colorimetric sensing system for glucose detection
Optical properties and peroxidase-like catalytic feature of Fe,Co-CQD
Sensing mechanism and detection of H2O2
Dual mode sensing of glucose at physiological pH
Visual detection of glucose by smartphone-assisted colorimetric sensing system

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Key Research and Development Program
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Key Research and Development Program of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors thank the financial support from the National Key Research and Development Program ( 2019YFC0312602 ) as well as the Jiangsu Overseas Visiting Scholar Program for University Prominent Young & Middle-aged Teachers and Presidents.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The synthetic route (A), XRD pattern (B), and TEM image (Inset: particle size distribution) (C) of Fe,Co-CQD. The XPS spectra of C 1s (D), N 1s (E), O 1s (F), Fe 2p (G), and Co 2p (H).

(A) The excitation and emission profiles (Inset: the images under natural light and 365 nm UV light). (B) The emission spectra under different excitation signals. (C) UV–vis absorption profiles of various systems (Inset is the relevant physical photo). (D) UV–vis absorption spectra of Fe,Co-CQD + H2O2 + chromogenic substrates (OPD, TMB, and ABTS) (Inset is the corresponding actual photograph).

(A) Sensing mechanism for H2O2 detection. (B) The emission spectrum of Fe,Co-CQD and UV–vis absorbance spectrum of DAP. (C) The fluorescence spectra of Fe,Co-CQD + OPD system with different contents of H2O2. (D) The related linear relationship between F555/F439 and H2O2 concentration. (E) The UV–vis absorption spectra of Fe,Co-CQD + OPD system with various levels of H2O2. (F) The related linear relationship between absorbance value at 420 nm and H2O2 level.

(A) Sensing mechanism for glucose detection at physiological pH. (B) The fluorescence spectra of Fe,Co-CQD + GOx + OPD system with different contents of glucose, and (C) the related linear relationship between F555/F439 and glucose concentration. (D) The UV–vis absorption spectra of Fe,Co-CQD + GOx + OPD system with various levels of glucose, and (E) the related linear relationship between absorbance value at 420 nm and glucose level. The fluorescence responses (F) and UV–vis responses (G) of glucose and various interferents toward Fe,Co-CQD + GOx + OPD system (The glucose concentration is 66.67 μM).

(A) Graphic illustration of smartphone-assisted colorimetric sensing strategy for glucose visual detection. (B) The related linear curve between ln(B) and glucose concentration (Inset is actual photo of chromogenic hydrogel spheres with various levels of glucose).

(A) Schematic diagram of dual-mode ratiometric fluorescence and colorimetric strategy for glucose analysis based on bifunctional Fe,Co-CQD. (B) Schematic illustration of smartphone-assisted colorimetric sensing system for blood glucose detection.

head.ce:title: A dual-mode ratiometric fluorescence and smartphone-assisted colorimetric sensing platform based on bifunctional Fe,Co-CQD for glucose analysis at physiological pH

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, School of Science, China Pharmaceutical University, Nanjing, 211198, Jiangsu, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, School of Science, China Pharmaceutical University, Nanjing 211198, Jiangsu, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Cell and Biomolecule Recognition Research Center

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Cell and Biomolecule Recognition Research Center, School of Science, China Pharmaceutical University, Nanjing 211198, Jiangsu, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Department of Chemistry, School of Science, China Pharmaceutical University, Nanjing, 211198, Jiangsu, PR China.
Corresponding author. Department of Chemistry, School of Science, China Pharmaceutical University, Nanjing, 211198, Jiangsu, PR China.

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Authors who equally contribute to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Diabetes is a common and fatal chronic metabolic disease characterized by hyperglycemia, and thus monitoring blood glucose level is essential for early screening and timely control of disease. Herein, we have prepared the bifunctional iron and cobalt co-doped carbon quantum dot (Fe,Co-CQD) with good optical properties and peroxidase-mimetic catalytic activity toward specific substrate o-phenylenediamine (OPD) under alkaline condition. Glucose oxidase (GOx) specifically catalyzes the oxidation of glucose into H2O2, and Fe,Co-CQD subsequently triggers a reaction between H2O2 and OPD to form yellow product DAP with a distinct UV absorption peak at 420 nm. Simultaneously, the generated DAP also appears a well-defined fluorescence signal at 555 nm, which can suppress the intrinsic fluorescence peak of Fe,Co-CQD (439 nm) owing to the inner filter effect (IFE). Based on this principle, a dual-mode ratiometric fluorescence and colorimetric sensing platform has been constructed for glucose analysis at physiological pH, which reveals the advantages of excellent accuracy, high throughput, simple operation, and low cost. More importantly, a smartphone-assisted colorimetric sensing system based on a portable visual detection kit and a 3D printing smartphone-based device has been constructed, which enables on-site detection of glucose in complex serum samples without laboratory instruments, indicating its potential practical application prospect.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Diabetes is a common and fatal chronic metabolic disease characterized by hyperglycemia, which can result in various complications such as kidney failure, stroke, and blindness [1,2]. The International Diabetes Federation (IDF) has reported that the global number of diabetes patients has risen to 463 million by 2019, and may reach 700 million by 2045 [3]. More seriously, about 1.6 million people die from diabetes every year according to the World Health Organization (WHO) [4]. Thus, monitoring the level of blood glucose is of vital importance for early screening and timely control of diabetes.

In the past decades, analytical strategies for rapid detection of blood glucose have been continuously established, including high-performance liquid chromatography [5], colorimetry [6–8], fluorometry [9], electrochemistry [10], and chemiluminescence [11]. Among them, numerous colorimetric or fluorescence biosensors have aroused great concern in view of their simple operation, low cost, and visual reading mode, which have been successfully explored and used for glucose sensing [12–14]. Unfortunately, the single-mode analysis strategies may lead to inaccurate or erroneous experimental results in practical applications owing to the influence of instrumental efficiency, operating procedure, and determination condition [15]. Surprisingly, the multi-mode sensing assays can significantly improve the accuracy of test results benefiting from its excellent anti-interference ability and self-calibration function [16]. In view of this, designing multi-mode optical biosensors for quantitative analysis of blood glucose is of great urgency. However, it is still an enormous challenge to construct a simple and reliable multi-mode sensing platform for efficient and rapid detection of glucose.

Advanced optical materials including quantum dots (QDs), rare earth materials, and metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) have captured widespread research interest because of their exceptional optical properties, inexpensive cost, and simple preparation [17]. In particular, carbon quantum dot (CQD), as a promising fluorescent nanomaterial, has been widely applied to biological imaging [18], cancer therapy [19], sensing [20], catalysis [21], and other fields. More interestingly, the doping of cobalt, iron, manganese, and other metal elements can endow CQDs with good enzyme-like activity, which can improve sensing performance and broaden application prospects of optical biosensors [22]. Notably, the bimetallic co-doped CQDs exhibit more functions or better physicochemical properties in comparison with single metal co-doped CQDs, which will be served as more favorable tools to develop new-type biochemical sensors [23,24].

Herein, we have synthesized iron and cobalt co-doped carbon quantum dot (Fe,Co-CQD) by a classical hydrothermal method. The developed Fe,Co-CQD appears peroxidase mimicking activity toward specific substrate o-phenylenediamine (OPD) under alkaline conditions. Further, the fluorescence intensity and peroxidase-like catalytic activity of Fe,Co-CQD can be regulated by adjusting the doped ratios of Fe and Co elements to construct a dual-mode biosensor with excellent performance. As depicted in Scheme 1 A, GOx can catalyze the oxidation of glucose to H2O2, and Fe,Co-CQD subsequently triggers a reaction between H2O2 and OPD with the solution system changing from colorless to yellow, and the formed DAP exhibits a distinct UV absorption peak at 420 nm. Simultaneously, the generated DAP also appears a well-defined fluorescence signal at 555 nm, which can suppress the intrinsic fluorescence peak of Fe,Co-CQD (439 nm) owing to the inner filter effect (IFE). Based on these principles, we have fabricated a dual-mode ratiometric fluorescence and colorimetric sensing strategy for blood glucose analysis at physiological pH. Moreover, a smartphone-assisted colorimetric sensing system has been designed and constructed for glucose detection more conveniently. As depicted in Scheme 1B, this sensing system includes a portable visual detection kit, a smartphone, and a 3D printing device. A colorimetric reaction is performed on the spherical hydrogel in the detection kit, and then the smartphone and spherical hydrogel are fixed by the 3D printing device. The color parameters (RGB values) of spherical hydrogel are measured by utilizing “Color Grab” application on smartphone and converted to the level of glucose after calibrating with the obtained standard curve. This simple colorimetric sensing system enables on-site detection of glucose without laboratory instruments, expanding the practical application prospect of nanosensors.
All materials and characterization are described in Supporting Information (SI).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of iron and cobalt co-doped carbon quantum dot (Fe,Co-CQD)
ce:section.ce:para: The detailed preparation process is provided in SI.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation on the peroxidase-like catalytic performance of Fe,Co-CQD
ce:section.ce:para: The OPD was selected as a chromogenic substrate to explore the peroxidase-like catalytic performance of Fe,Co-CQD. In brief, 100 μL Fe,Co-CQD and 2700 μL Tris-HCl buffer (50 mM, pH 7.5) were transferred into a 5 mL centrifuge tube. Subsequently, 100 μL H2O2 (1 mM) and 100 μL OPD (20 mM) were added into the above mixture. After incubation at 37 °C for 20 min, UV absorbance values at 420 nm were recorded by Purkinje T6 spectrophotometer (China).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric and fluorescence detection of H2O2 and glucose
ce:section.ce:para: For H2O2 sensing, 100 μL Fe,Co-CQD and 2700 μL Tris-HCl buffer were transferred into a 5 mL centrifuge tube. Subsequently, 100 μL H2O2 (a series of different concentrations) and 100 μL OPD (20 mM) were introduced into the above mixture. After reaction at 37 °C for 20 min, the fluorescence signals at 439 nm and 555 nm were recorded by fluorescence spectrophotometer with the excitation signal of 375 nm, and UV absorption values at 420 nm were measured by Purkinje T6 spectrophotometer.

For glucose detection, 100 μL GOx (7 mg mL−1) and 100 μL glucose (dissolved into Tris-HCl buffer, pH 7.5) were mixed together, and then incubated at a 37 °C thermostat water bath for 25 min. Subsequently, 100 μL Fe,Co-CQD, 100 μL OPD (20 mM), and 2600 μL Tris-HCl buffer were added into the above mixture, and then reacted at 37 °C for 20 min. Finally, the fluorescence signals at 439 and 555 nm were observed, and absorption values at 420 nm were determined by UV–vis spectrophotometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of portable detection kits and smartphone-assisted colorimetric sensing system for glucose detection
ce:section.ce:para: Portable glucose detection kits were prepared as follows: 80 μL Fe,Co-CQD, 450 μL Tris-HCl buffer (pH 7.5), 20 μL GOx (10 mg mL−1), and a spherical hydrogel (about 2 mm in diameter) were added into a 5 mL plastic centrifuge tube. After soaking for several hours, the liquid in the centrifuge tube was completely absorbed, and the diameter of spherical hydrogel expanded to ∼0.9 cm. Subsequently, they were stored at 4 °C for further use. When the detection kit was used for glucose sensing, a series of various concentrations of 20 μL glucose (dissolved into Tris-HCl buffer, pH 7.5) and 30 μL OPD (20 mM) were introduced into prepared detection kit and incubated together at 37 °C for 45 min. Subsequently, the smartphone and spherical hydrogel were fixed by the 3D printing device which was designed by Rhinoceros 5 software. Finally, the color parameters (RGB values) of spherical hydrogel were captured by “Color Grab” application on smartphone and converted to the level of glucose after calibrating with the obtained standard curve.

The relevant properties of undoped CQDs are first explored. As displayed in Fig. S1, the undoped CQDs show general fluorescence intensity and negligible peroxidase-like activity. Therefore, metal doping is very important to improve and regulate the performance of CQDs. To investigate the effects of different doped ratios of Fe and Co elements on intrinsic fluorescence intensity and peroxidase-like activity of CQDs, the CQDs with different doped ratios of Fe and Co elements were synthesized (Table S1). The intrinsic fluorescence intensities of the prepared CQDs gradually strengthen with the increase of doping ratio of Fe (Fig. S2), while their peroxidase-like activities gradually weaken with the decrease of Co doping ratio (Fig. S3). To balance the fluorescence intensity and peroxidase-like activity of the obtained CQDs to develop a ratiometric fluorescence sensor with excellent performance, the CQD with iron cobalt ratio of 0.25:0.75 was selected for the following experiments and named as Fe,Co-CQD (Fig. S4). As shown in Fig. 1 A, Fe,Co-CQD was prepared through facile hydrothermal route [25]. The XRD spectrum (Fig. 1B) reveals the structure of amorphous carbon similar to some reported metal-doped CQDs, and the appearance of a sharp peak at 29.4° should be attributed to the doping of iron [26,27]. The TEM image displays near spherical small particle with average particle size of about 3.5 nm (Fig. 1C).

The surface groups information on Fe,Co-CQD was acquired by FT-IR analysis. As demonstrated by Fig. S5, the stretching vibration peaks of O–H and N–H appear at 3525 and 3221 cm−1, respectively. The peaks at 1655 and 1385 cm−1 originate from CO and C–N stretching vibration [28]. To further confirm the chemical bond and elemental composition of Fe,Co-CQD, the XPS analysis was performed. The XPS scanning full spectrum (Fig. S6) demonstrates the presence of C, N, O, Fe, and Co elements in the prepared Fe,Co-CQD. As depicted in the high-resolution C 1s spectrum (Fig. 1D), the peaks at 284.9, 286.3, and 288.3 eV should be attributed to C–C/CC, C–O/C–N, and CO bonds, respectively. The N 1s spectrum (Fig. 1E) displays two peaks at 399.9 and 401.7 eV, corresponding to C–N/CN and N–H groups, respectively. The O 1s spectrum (Fig. 1F) consists of two peaks at 531.8 and 532.9 eV, which are attributed to CO and C–O, respectively [26]. Moreover, the Fe 2p spectrum (Fig. 1G) reveals two peaks at 715.3 and 723.8 eV, owing to Fe 2p3/2 and Fe 2p1/2, respectively [25]. The Co 2p spectrum (Fig. 1H) comprises Co 2p3/2 (781.2 eV) and Co 2p1/2 (796.9 eV) with two satellite peaks at 785.5 and 802.2 eV, respectively, suggesting the existence of Co(II) [22].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties and peroxidase-like catalytic feature of Fe,Co-CQD
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the optical properties of Fe,Co-CQD, the correlative fluorescence experiments were carried out. As depicted in Fig. 2 A, optimal excitation and emission signals of Fe,Co-CQD are observed at 345 and 427 nm, respectively. Evidently, Fe,Co-CQD aqueous solution exhibits apparent blue light under 365 nm excitation (Fig. 2A). Consistent with some reported CQDs, Fe,Co-CQD reveals excitation-dependent fluorescence behaviors [29,30]. The fluorescence intensity gradually diminishes and emission wavelength shifts significantly with the excitation wavelength away from 345 nm (Fig. 2B).

The peroxidase-like catalytic feature of Fe,Co-CQD was evaluated by using OPD as chromogenic substrate in the existence of H2O2. As observed from Fig. 2C, a visible color reaction from colorless to yellow is occurred in the Fe,Co-CQD + H2O2 + OPD system, leading to a distinct absorbance peak at 420 nm. However, no evident color changes are observed in other systems. This comparison indicates that Fe,Co-CQD has intrinsic peroxidase-like activity, which can catalyze oxidation of OPD to its product DAP in the existence of H2O2. Similar to other nanozymes, the enzyme-like activity of Fe,Co-CQD is highly dependent on buffer pH [12,31]. As presented in Fig. S7, the Fe,Co-CQD reveals good catalytic activity toward substrate OPD under alkaline conditions and hardly any catalytic activity under acidic and neutral conditions, which is diverse from the characteristic of most reported nanozymes [32–35]. Furthermore, Fe,Co-CQD exhibits optimal catalytic activity at pH 8 and still reveals 57.3% catalytic activity at near physiological pH (7.5), which is conducive to blood glucose detection under physiological pH. Surprisingly, Fe,Co-CQD displays catalytic selectivity toward chromogenic substrates. As shown in Fig. 2D, no clear color changes are seen in Fe,Co-CQD + H2O2 + TMB (ABTS) systems and no distinct characteristic peaks appear at 417 (ABTSox) or 652 nm (TMBox) when two other typical chromogenic substrates (ABTS, TMB) were used under physiological pH. Besides, Fe,Co-CQD still fails to exhibit catalytic activity toward TMB and ABTS when the pH buffers are varied from 3 to 10 (Fig. S8). To explore the mechanism of this phenomenon, the surface charge values of Fe,Co-CQD in different pH buffers were measured by zeta potentiometer. The surface of Fe,Co-CQD exhibits more negative charges under alkaline conditions (Fig. S9), which is conducive to attracting the positively charged OPD substrate to its surface for catalytic reaction via electrostatic interaction. These results demonstrate that Fe,Co-CQD specifically reveals peroxidase-mimetic activity toward OPD under alkaline conditions.

To further investigate peroxidase-mimetic activity of Fe,Co-CQD, the typical Michaelis-Menten curves toward OPD and H2O2 were obtained by steady-state kinetic experiments (Fig. S10). Michaelis-Menten constant (K
m ) and maximum reaction velocity (V
max ) of Fe,Co-CQD were calculated and listed in Table S2. As demonstrated by Table S2, the K
m values of Fe,Co-CQD toward substrates OPD and H2O2 are lower than that of horseradish peroxidase (HRP) [36], testifying the better affinity of Fe,Co-CQD toward OPD and H2O2 compared with HRP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing mechanism and detection of H2O2
ce:section.ce:para: In view of bifunctional Fe,Co-CQD with good fluorescent property and peroxidase-mimetic activity, a dual-mode ratiometric fluorescence and colorimetric sensing platform can be fabricated for H2O2 detection at physiological pH. As illustrated in Fig. 3 A, Fe,Co-CQD can catalyze oxidation of OPD to generate yellow product DAP with a distinct UV absorption peak at 420 nm in the existence of H2O2, and the generated DAP also appears a well-defined fluorescence signal at 555 nm, which can suppress the intrinsic fluorescence peak of Fe,Co-CQD (439 nm). To investigate the principle of this appearance, both emission spectrum of Fe,Co-CQD and UV–vis absorption spectrum of DAP were tested. As depicted in Fig. 3B, the emission spectrum of Fe,Co-CQD evidently overlaps with the absorption spectrum of DAP, verifying that the fluorescence suppression phenomenon should be attributed to the inner filter effect (IFE) [37,38]. Therefore, dual-mode ratiometric fluorescence and colorimetric analysis of H2O2 can be achieved at physiological pH based on fluorescence intensity ratio of DAP and Fe,Co-CQD (F555/F439) and typical UV absorption peak of DAP (420 nm).

To realize optimal analysis performance, some operating parameters such as excitation wavelength and reaction time were optimized in this experiment. As confirmed by Fig. S11, the fluorescence intensity of Fe,Co-CQD gradually decreased, while that of formed DAP significantly increased with the increase of excitation wavelength. Consequently, the excitation wavelength is set as 375 nm in consideration of the sensitivity and linearity range of ratiometric fluorescence sensor. Additionally, the fluorescence intensity ratio of DAP and Fe,Co-CQD (F555/F439) increases as the reaction time extends. In view of the rapid sensing of H2O2, 20 min is chosen as the reaction time for the following experiments (Fig. S12).

Under the optimal conditions, the fluorescence spectra of Fe,Co-CQD + OPD system with various levels of H2O2 were recorded and displayed in Fig. 3C. The fluorescence peak at 439 nm decreases with the increasing content of H2O2, while fluorescence peak at 555 nm gradually increases (Fig. 3C). Interestingly, a good linear relationship between F555/F439 and H2O2 level is obtained and the linear fitting equation is F555/F439 = 0.0291[H2O2] + 0.2921 (R2 = 0.996) in the range of 0.17–33.33 μM (Fig. 3D). The limit of detection (LOD) is calculated to be 0.093 μM according to the signal-to-noise of three (S/N = 3). Furthermore, the UV absorption peak at 420 nm increases progressively with the addition of H2O2 (Fig. 3E). The good linear relationship between absorbance value of 420 nm (A420) and H2O2 level in the scope of 0.67–33.33 μM can be observed, offering a linear equation: A420 = 0.0029[H2O2] + 0.0361 with a regression coefficient of 0.997, and the limit of detection (LOD) is calculated as low as 0.437 μM on the basis of S/N = 3 (Fig. 3F). Compared with other reported methods (Table S3), the linear range and LOD of developed dual-mode sensor based on Fe,Co-CQD for H2O2 analysis are comparable or even superior to other currently reported sensors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dual mode sensing of glucose at physiological pH
ce:section.ce:para: Since the catalytic activity of nanozyme is highly dependent on the system pH, most nanozyme-based biosensors can only be applied for analytes sensing under acidic or near-neutral conditions, which limits their application prospects in the biochemical field [12]. Considering that prepared Fe,Co-CQD reveals good peroxidase-mimetic activity toward specific substrate OPD at physiological pH, here we have fabricated a dual-mode ratiometric fluorescence and colorimetric sensing platform for blood glucose analysis under physiological conditions (Fig. 4 A). This strategy can avoid the adjustment of buffer pH during the detection process, which simplifies the operation steps and improves the analysis accuracy. To achieve the optimum analytical performance of developed sensing platform, the optimal GOx concentration and incubation time were investigated. As demonstrated by Fig. S13 and Fig. S14, GOx concentration and incubation time are set as 7 mg mL−1 and 25 min, respectively. Subsequently, the fabricated dual-mode sensing platform are used for blood glucose detection under optimized experimental conditions. As confirmed by Fig. 4B, the fluorescence intensity at 439 nm reduces with the increasing glucose content in the scope of 0.33─60 μM, while the fluorescence peak at 555 nm increases reversely. The linear fitting diagram of glucose level and F555/F439 is displayed in Fig. 4C, and the linear fitting equation can be recorded as F555/F439 = 0.0206[glucose] + 0.3216 (R2 = 0.999) with a LOD of 0.167 μM (S/N = 3). Furthermore, the absorbance signal at 420 nm gradually increases with the increase of glucose concentration (Fig. 4D), and a linear equation for the colorimetric method in the range of 3.33─100 μM with a LOD of 1.126 μM (S/N = 3) is acquired (Fig. 4E), which can be described as A420 = 0.0020[glucose] + 0.0432 (R2 = 0.998). As compared in Table S4, compared with other reported strategies, the designed dual-mode sensing platform based on Fe,Co-CQD for glucose detection is comparable or even better in respect of linear range and LOD. More significantly, this sensing platform enables dual-mode detection of glucose under physiological pH conditions, which is of great significance to the field of biochemical analysis.

To investigate the selectivity of the fabricated dual-mode sensing platform based on bifunctional Fe,Co-CQD for glucose detection, various common species were selected for experiments. No evident effects were observed in fluorescence and colorimetric modes even if the interferent concentration is about five times of glucose level (Fig. 4F and G), demonstrating the good specificity of dual-mode sensing platform for glucose analysis. To evaluate the practicability of the proposed dual-mode sensing platform, the glucose in human serum samples was detected by spike and recovery experiment. First, the human serum samples were centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min to obtain the supernatant, which was then diluted 50-fold with ultrapure water. Subsequently, the diluted serum samples were mixed with different concentrations of glucose to prepare spiked samples. Finally, the glucose levels in diluted serum and spiked samples were measured through fabricated dual-mode sensing platform, and the detection results are shown in Table S5. The recoveries ranges from 92.1% to 108.6%, manifesting the good reliability and robustness of the fabricated dual-mode sensing platform in the sensing of complex actual samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visual detection of glucose by smartphone-assisted colorimetric sensing system
ce:section.ce:para: To realize more portable glucose sensing, a smartphone-assisted colorimetric sensing system which contains a portable visual detection kit, a smartphone, and a 3D printing device was constructed, and the relevant operation processes are described in Fig. 5 A. As presented in Fig. S15, the device with the overall dimensions of 100 mm (l) × 100 mm (w) × 190 mm (h) mainly comprises three parts: (I) The top part contains phone holder and light-shied board; (II) The middle part is used to adjust the focal length between the smartphone camera and the hydrogel by changing the number of embedded boards; (III) The bottom part is drawer-type 9-well plate to hold the hydrogel. Additionally, the relevant color parameters (RGB values) in the reaction system are recorded through “Color Grab” application on the smartphone. Amazingly, an acceptable linear dependence between the logarithm of Blue (B) color channel value and glucose levels ranging from 6.7 to 333.3 μM is observed (Fig. 5B), and the regression equation is ln(B) = −0.0018[glucose] + 5.3861 (R2 = 0.992) with a LOD of 2.6 μM (S/N = 3). To verify the reliability and practicability of smartphone-assisted colorimetric sensing system, we utilized it to analyze glucose in serum samples by spike and recovery experiment. As depicted in Table S6, acceptable recoveries can be observed through the colorimetric sensing system, demonstrating its feasibility for glucose detection in complex actual samples.

In summary, we have developed the bifunctional Fe,Co-CQD with considerable optical properties and peroxidase-mimetic catalytic activity toward specific substrate OPD under alkaline condition, through which a dual-mode ratiometric fluorescence and colorimetric sensing platform can be constructed for glucose analysis at physiological pH. This dual-mode sensing platform has good sensing performance and exhibits acceptable accuracy and stability even in complex serum samples. More importantly, an interesting idea is to establish a smartphone-assisted colorimetric sensing system based on a portable visual detection kit and a 3D printing smartphone-based device, which provides a favorable tool for on-site detection of glucose and even other analytes without laboratory instruments.

Linjie Wang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Shujun Zheng: Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Validation, Writing – original draft. Ling Lu: Formal analysis, Validation. Caolong Li: Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Fei Wang: Resources, Visualization, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank the financial support from the National Key Research and Development Program (2019YFC0312602) as well as the Jiangsu Overseas Visiting Scholar Program for University Prominent Young & Middle-aged Teachers and Presidents.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2022.340701.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Sulfur vacancy promoted peroxidase-like activity of magnetic greigite (Fe3S4) for colorimetric detection of serum glucose
Anal. Chim. Acta

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. Liu, J. Tian, C. Mao, Z. Wang, J. Liu, R.A. Dahlgren, L. Zhang, X. Wang, Sulfur vacancy promoted peroxidase-like activity of magnetic greigite (Fe3S4) for colorimetric detection of serum glucose, Anal. Chim. Acta 1127 (2020) 246-255.
